mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable LLM serving with advanced features like quantization and continuous batching. Key trade-offs include the need for specific hardware optimizations and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the vLLM Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install vLLM easily.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.19.1

```

> This adds the specific vLLM repository for version 0.19.1.

steps.title: Install vLLM CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for vLLM to function properly in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.19.1/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

```

> Ensure CRDs are installed before deploying the application.

steps.title: Install vLLM using Helm
steps.description:
This command installs the vLLM application in a specified namespace, setting up all necessary resources.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.19.1

```

> This installs vLLM in the 'vllm' namespace.

steps.title: Create an example Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates an example Custom Resource (CR) for vLLM, which defines the model and its configuration.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  parameters:
    max_length: 512
EOF

```

> This creates a sample vLLM instance with a specified model.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  parameters:
    max_length: 512
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Custom Resource is ready
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the vLLM instance to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get vllm example-vllm -o yaml

```

> Look for 'status: ready' in the output to confirm successful deployment.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step removes the vLLM installation and associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss and orphaned resources.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command removes the vLLM release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data. This process involves draining the existing pods, upgrading the Helm release, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.19.1

kubectl get vllm example-vllm -o yaml

```

> Replace <node-name> with the actual node name.
> This command upgrades vLLM to the specified version.
> Verify the status of the vLLM instance after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'vllm not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM Helm chart may not be added correctly.

**Fix:**
Re-add the Helm repository using 'helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.19.1'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'CRD already exists'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) were previously installed.

**Fix:**
Check existing CRDs with 'kubectl get crd' and delete if necessary using 'kubectl delete crd <crd-name>'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM instance may be misconfigured or the model may not be available.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' for details and verify the model configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources allocated for vLLM.

**Fix:**
Ensure your cluster has at least 2 vCPU and 4GB RAM per node, and consider scaling up your nodes.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new quantization methods and improved model serving efficiency.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the vLLM instance with status 'ready' when running 'kubectl get vllm example-vllm'.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources and the necessary tools installed.
